Inser element to linked list
fields.body_text: To insert an element (node) into a singly linked list, you change pointer references so the existing nodes link to the new node. You can insert a node at three primary locations: the beginning, the end, or a specific middle position.

fields.title: Procedural vs. Object-Oriented Programming
fields.body_text:
Procedural Programming focuses on writing sequential functions or blocks of code that carry out tasks.
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) organizes data and behavior into reusable structures called objects.The core differences between the two paradigms are detailed below:
Core Methodology
Procedural: Top-down approach where a program is broken down into small functions.
OOP: Bottom-up approach where data and functions are bound together into objects.
Data Security
Procedural: Data moves openly around the system from function to function.
OOP: Data is hidden through encapsulation and can only be accessed via specific object methods.
Code Reusability 
Procedural: Very low reusability, leading to highly redundant code structures.
OOP: High reusability achieved through inheritance, polymorphism, and class blueprints.
Modification & Scaling
Procedural: Hard to modify or expand because changing data affects the whole program.
OOP: Easy to scale since adding new objects does not break existing code.

fields.title: The Four Pillars of OOP
fields.body_text:
The true power of OOP comes from four fundamental principles:

Encapsulation: The practice of bundling data (variables) and methods (functions) together into a single unit. It allows you to restrict direct access to some of an object's components, protecting internal data from unauthorized changes.

Abstraction: The concept of hiding complex implementation details and exposing only the necessary features to the user (e.g., using the brake pedal of a car without needing to know how the hydraulic fluid works).

Inheritance: A mechanism where a new class (derived or child class) takes on the properties and methods of an existing class (base or parent class). This prevents code duplication and promotes reusability.

Polymorphism: The ability of different objects to respond uniquely to the same method call. For instance, a makeSound() method might trigger a "Meow" for a cat object and a "Bark" for a dog object.

fields.title: Moving away from simple questions to using structured frameworks like CO-STAR
fields.body_text:
Step-by-Step Breakdown:
Setting a clear Persona (e.g., "Act as a veteran product marketing manager").
Defining the Target Audience to tailor the complexity of the output.
Adding negative constraints (e.g., "Do not use corporate jargon or buzzwords").

Hands-on Example:
Bad Prompt: "Write an email about our new app."
Powerful Prompt: "Act as a copywriter.Write a 150-word product launch email for busy parents. Highlight how our app saves 2 hours a week on meal planning. Use an encouraging tone. Format with 3 bullet points."

fields.question_text: Under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which of the following best describes the requirement for valid user consent?
A data analyst intentionally leaves out outlier data points from a report to make the project results look more successful. What ethical principle did they violate?
